does: This paper introduces PreSa, a method for offline preference-based reinforcement learning that directly learns safe policies from pairwise preference comparisons and binary safety labels without explicitly learning separate reward and cost models. Rather than the standard two-stage approach of first fitting reward and cost functions then applying constrained RL, the method directly optimizes a policy using preference data about behavioral quality alongside trajectory-level safety annotations. The approach addresses the problem of error accumulation in long-horizon continuous control tasks, where indirect learning through separate models can compound mistakes when used with constrained RL optimization.
connects: This work extends the growing body of research combining preference-based learning with safety constraints in offline RL. It builds on preference-based RL methods that learn from pairwise comparisons without hand-crafted rewards, while also responding to recent efforts in safe RLHF that incorporate safety considerations alongside reward learning. The paper addresses limitations of the sequential approach used in LLM fine-tuning (learning reward and cost models first, then constrained optimization), which has been shown to be problematic in long-horizon control domains. It connects to both offline RL literature dealing with distributional shift and constrained MDPs that formalize safety as constraint satisfaction, proposing a unified framework that avoids the pitfalls of separate model learning.
enables: This framework opens directions for more robust safe policy learning in domains requiring both performance and safety guarantees without extensive human supervision. By avoiding separate reward and cost estimation, the method could enable better scaling of preference-based learning to complex continuous control tasks in robotics, autonomous systems, and other safety-critical applications. The direct policy learning approach could facilitate future work on combining diverse types of human feedback signals beyond binary preferences and safety labels, such as trajectory rankings or safety criticality scores. The framework also points toward designing algorithms that directly optimize for safety-performance trade-offs, potentially enabling better uncertainty quantification and robustness in offline safe RL settings.
